title: How China Is Winning Friends and Influencing People
gist: China's private companies, not Beijing's state propaganda, are winning genuine goodwill across the developing world by localizing cheap, well-adapted products, but the same domestic overcapacity and price wars driving this expansion are eroding Chinese firms' profits and innovation capacity while triggering a growing wave of host-country protectionism.

insights.claim: China's private companies, driven by profit rather than state strategy, are generating positive global perceptions of China that Beijing's official top-down efforts — state media, propaganda, cultural exchanges, language programs — have failed to achieve.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: China's soft-power gains are a bottom-up byproduct of commercial competition, not a controllable state asset — don't extrapolate from it to predict coordinated Chinese geopolitical influence campaigns.
insights.locator: The omnipresence of Chinese products is expanding the country's influence

insights.claim: Most of Beijing's Confucius Institutes in Australia, Europe, and North America have been shuttered over the past decade amid concerns about academic freedom and lack of transparency, marking the failure of top-down cultural diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: State-led Chinese cultural diplomacy is in structural retreat in the West even as commercial influence grows elsewhere.
insights.locator: Beijing tried to promote Chinese language and culture

insights.claim: Coercive Chinese tactics abroad — pressuring critics and censoring dissenting views — have backfired, as when Chinese pressure forced organizers to suspend New York's IndieChina Film Festival in 2025.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Overt Chinese state pressure abroad tends to generate backlash, reinforcing that unbranded commercial penetration is Beijing's more effective influence channel.
insights.locator: More coercive tactics, including pressuring critics

insights.claim: When Beijing tries to inject overt political messaging into organic cultural exports — such as covertly paying influencers around the 2022 Winter Olympics, or banning the popular 'boys' love' costume-drama genre in 2021 for 'abnormal aesthetics' — it undermines rather than promotes its influence, because bottom-up authenticity is what makes Chinese soft power resonate, especially with digitally native youth.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Any Chinese state attempt to formalize or nationalize its commercial soft-power success is likely to weaken it — a durable constraint on Beijing's influence strategy.
insights.locator: Beijing has also tried to package political propaganda

insights.claim: In 2025, China exported roughly $1.6 trillion of goods to developing economies — about 50 percent more than it sold to the US and western Europe combined — with private firms accounting for nearly 60 percent of China's total foreign trade.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: The developing world, not the US or Europe, is now China's primary export growth engine — reorient China-demand analysis away from G7 trade data alone.
insights.locator: In 2025, China exported roughly $1.6 trillion

insights.claim: Chinese brands — Hisense in South Africa, Xiaomi and the milk-tea chain Mixue in Brazil, Yadea scooters and Chinese EV brands in Vietnam — are becoming as ubiquitous in the developing world as American brands like Levi's, Marlboro, and McDonald's were in the 1980s and 1990s, while most American products remain out of reach for ordinary consumers.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: China is capturing the mass-market consumer-brand position the US held for decades in emerging markets, a durable structural shift in global consumer share.
insights.locator: In South Africa, Hisense televisions

insights.claim: Product ubiquity itself shapes perception: as Chinese goods, apps, and infrastructure become part of daily life, they begin to define how people imagine competence, modernity, and possibility, mirroring the effect of American products' dominance for over half a century.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Brand ubiquity in emerging markets is a leading indicator of longer-term consumer and political alignment, independent of any government messaging.
insights.locator: As Chinese companies expand, the country is becoming associated

insights.claim: Shenzhen-based phone maker Transsion won the African market by designing hardware global brands ignored: batteries suited to unreliable electricity grids, dual-SIM capability for fragmented telecom markets, and camera software recalibrated for darker skin tones, unlike leading global brands whose cameras were optimized for lighter complexions.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Transsion's local-first product design is a repeatable playbook other Chinese firms are copying to win price-sensitive emerging markets that Western brands have neglected.
insights.locator: Consider Transsion, a phone company based in the tech hub

insights.claim: Chinese microdrama platforms DramaBox and ShortMax have captured Southeast Asian audiences — over 300 overseas streaming apps now operate in the region with more than a billion downloads combined — by avoiding the romantic content common in US shows, which plays better in culturally conservative Muslim-majority markets like Indonesia and Malaysia, while localizing via dubbing and local actors.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Chinese short-form entertainment is displacing US content as the default option in culturally conservative emerging markets, a durable media-consumption shift.
insights.locator: In Southeast Asia, Chinese dramas and short-form vertical videos

insights.claim: Chinese firms create a 'Walmart effect' in developing markets by pricing goods for consumers with limited discretionary income, normalizing Chinese products and expanding access to goods previously unattainable for lower-income shoppers.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Price-point innovation, not just subsidy-driven dumping, is a core and durable source of Chinese firms' developing-market share gains.
insights.locator: Most important, Chinese brands have been more effective

themes.theme: Involution: the domestic engine forcing firms abroad

insights.claim: China's oversaturated domestic market is generating 'involution' — ruinous price wars that push firms to increase output to cut costs, eroding profits and starving long-term R&D investment.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Involution is the key mental model for why Chinese firms are expanding abroad aggressively even as their domestic margins collapse.
insights.locator: Within China, firms face what is known domestically as involution

insights.claim: Weak Chinese domestic demand — driven by depleted pandemic-era household savings, a property market that began declining in 2021, and high youth unemployment — is intensifying involution and pushing firms to seek growth overseas.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: China's overseas commercial expansion is a symptom of unresolved domestic demand weakness, not a sign of underlying economic strength.
insights.locator: Involution has become even more severe in the past few years

insights.claim: Tariffs, antisubsidy investigations, data-security scrutiny, and investment restrictions in Europe, Japan, South Korea, and the US have narrowed Chinese firms' access to advanced-economy markets, redirecting their expansion toward Africa, Latin America, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Advanced-economy trade barriers are directly redirecting, not stopping, Chinese export flows toward the developing world.
insights.locator: Chinese firms' access to markets in advanced industrial economies

insights.claim: Chinese firms succeeding in the developing world are not simply dumping surplus product, since they face intense competition against other subsidized Chinese firms in the same overseas markets — meaning only the most locally adaptive companies survive, making localization rather than price alone the winning strategy.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: The common 'China is just dumping excess supply' narrative undersells the genuine product-market fit Chinese firms have built, which makes their gains stickier than a pure price story would imply.
insights.locator: Still, Chinese companies have not succeeded in expanding

insights.claim: The same price-war dynamics making Chinese firms globally competitive are undermining China's long-term innovation capacity, since firms with thin margins have no cash cushion to invest in R&D or build durable brands.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: China's developing-world commercial success and its stated ambition to become an innovation superpower are in direct tension — a structural headwind for the highest-value segments of Chinese industry.
insights.locator: But the same competitive dynamics that are driving Chinese companies

insights.claim: The Chinese solar panel industry illustrates the trap: despite dominating global manufacturing and facing high global demand, severe overcapacity and cutthroat pricing have produced industrywide losses that threaten sustained R&D investment.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Solar is a live case study of a Chinese sector with dominant market share but eroding profitability — a template that could repeat in EVs and batteries.
insights.locator: The solar panel industry is an example of this challenge

insights.claim: Only a handful of large, cash-rich firms such as BYD have the balance sheet to keep investing through the price war; most Chinese companies operate with little financial cushion, limiting the sector's capacity for durable, long-term international success.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Expect increasing bifurcation between a few well-capitalized Chinese national champions and a long tail of firms unable to sustain overseas expansion.
insights.locator: Although a few large firms, such as BYD

insights.claim: Cheap Chinese imports are squeezing local manufacturers and locking developing countries into low-value segments of industry; in Thailand alone, nearly 2,000 factories closed between 2023 and 2024 under pressure from Chinese imports combined with rising energy costs.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Local industrial hollowing-out in developing markets is a measurable, near-term cost of China's export strategy and a source of rising trade friction.
insights.locator: In Thailand, for instance, nearly 2,000 factories closed

insights.claim: A wave of developing-country protectionism against China is underway: Vietnam and Indonesia have filed antidumping measures on steel and ceramics, Brazil raised tariffs on electric vehicles, Mexico's Congress approved tariff hikes on more than 1,400 products, and South Africa introduced new certification requirements on Chinese generators and solar panels.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Trade-defense measures against China are spreading well beyond the US and EU into the developing world itself — a broadening headwind for Chinese export growth.
insights.locator: Many are already doing so

insights.claim: China's commercial soft-power gains are structurally fragile: they boost China's image at the individual consumer level but generate macro-level tension with governments and industries that see a new form of economic dependence taking hold.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Don't extrapolate consumer-level Chinese popularity into stable government-level relationships — the two are diverging.
insights.locator: This backlash reveals the promise and limits of China's growing influence

insights.claim: The US has no comparable answer to China's developing-world commercial penetration, since American firms show little interest in expanding into low-margin, price-sensitive, politically fragile markets.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: Expect the US to keep losing developing-world consumer market share to China by default rather than through any active counter-strategy.
insights.locator: The United States is ill equipped to counter the influence

insights.claim: If Chinese firms' overseas success continues to relieve the pressure that would otherwise force Beijing to fix its underlying growth model, it becomes a pyrrhic victory that entrenches China's low-productivity, export-led growth pattern rather than resolving it.
insights.speaker: Lizzi C. Lee
insights.so_what: China's export success abroad may be delaying, not enabling, the domestic rebalancing needed for a genuine transition to higher-productivity growth.
insights.locator: Even if Beijing stays out of the way
